data.title: Trade Turmoil Creates Opportunity - Municipal April Update
data.description:
By Patrick Haskell | James Schwartz | Sean Carney

Market overview

Municipal bonds posted negative total returns in April as the announcement of reciprocal tariffs early in the month spurred extreme volatility across global markets. The asset class, though primarily supported by domestic revenue, was dragged down by rich valuations, challenged supply-and-demand dynamics, and constrained liquidity, and posted four of the ten largest daily sell-offs in the past 20 years during the week of April 7. Fortunately, market conditions stabilized following President Trump’s announcement of a 90-day pause on most tariffs, and some losses were mitigated. The S&P Municipal Bond Index ultimately returned –0.56%, bringing the year-to-date total return to – 0.80%. The front end of the yield curve, AAA-rated credits, pre-refunded bonds, and the state GO, special tax, and housing sectors performed best.

Market technicals deteriorated quickly amid the market turmoil. Demand, while already waning as a result of typical seasonal tax-time weakness, turned staunchly more negative as volatility increased and performance plummeted. Daily bid-wanted activity swelled as a result, hitting the second-highest level of all time amid limited liquidity. At the same time, issuance remained robust at $53 billion and outpaced reinvestment income from maturities, calls, and coupons by $25 billion. However, the cadence of issuance was severely impacted, with numerous deals delayed due to market conditions.

Municipal fundamentals remain largely unchanged, and as the market continues to normalize, we see favorable opportunities at today’s adjusted absolute and relative valuations. Consequently, we are cautiously aggressive, ensuring we maintain appropriate cash balances while also adding attractive bonds at higher yields.

Strategy insights

We have shifted to long duration. We favor an up-in-quality bias given a more attractive opportunity set following the recent market correction. We advocate a barbell yield curve strategy, pairing front-end exposure with the 15–20-year part of the curve. We are increasingly selective in sectors with outsized policy risks, including higher education and healthcare.

Overweight

  • Suburban governments and school districts
  • Prepaid gas
  • Housing bonds
  • Select issuers in the high yield space
  • National and large regional health systems

Underweight

  • Speculative projects with weak sponsorship, unproven technology, or unsound feasibility studies
  • Rural, exurban, and urban safety net hospitals
  • Senior living and long-term care facilities
  • Small private colleges

Credit headlines

In April, the Trump administration demanded changes to Harvard University's governance, hiring, admissions, and academic audits to keep its federal funding. Accused of discriminatory actions against Jewish students that cited Title VI violations, the university rejected these demands, claiming they exceed government authority and threaten the school's independence. The administration then froze $2.2 billion in grants and $60 million in contracts, putting at risk $9 billion in research funding, and threatened to revoke Harvard's tax-exempt status, potentially imposing a $465 million annual tax bill. Additional threats included banning foreign students and requesting detailed records of their activities. Experts believe the tax code protects taxpayers from executive branch-directed audits, leaving the courts to resolve the dispute. Other universities that do not comply with the administration’s mandates may face similar challenges.

Harvard's tax-exempt status threat has impacted prices for their $3.3 billion tax-exempt bonds. The university's AAA-rated tax-exempt bonds have cheapened in recent weeks, as risk-averse investors prefer to avoid the headlines from the escalating political clash. Despite selling pressures, most investors see a buying opportunity due to Harvard's $53 billion endowment, strong financial operations, inelastic demand from prospective students, and a lengthy tax-exemption revocation process with potential legal battles.

data.title: Marriott International: It Already Had A Great Run, So Wait For Now (Downgrade)
data.description:
Marriott International, Inc. (NASDAQ: MAR) proves it can still deliver a decent performance amid macroeconomic uncertainties and shifting travel perceptions. Its well-diversified business portfolio and improved metrics remain its core strength. However, it must beware of its high financial leverage and the weakening travel trends, which can affect its performance. There are also potential overpricing and technical risks to watch out for. We will discuss these aspects one by one in this new coverage.

MAR Q1 2025: A Good Start

Marriott started 2025 with a notable Q1 performance despite the looming challenges and macroeconomic risks. Its operating revenue amounted to $6.26B, up by 4.7% YoY from $5.98B. Its fee-based business segment remained the cornerstone of its operations, increasing by 3.1% YoY. Although its incentive management fees weakened, base management and franchise fees completely offset them. Their combined amount of $1.07B rose by 7.0%. Cost reimbursements also supported revenue growth as its amount went up by 5.0%.

Meanwhile, its owned and leased hotels remained relatively flat, and somehow it was a laggard during the quarter. As we can see in its report, its revenue growth of only 1.1% was easily offset by the 3.4% increase in expenses in this segment. Nonetheless, it did not mar MAR’s growth and profitability.

MAR capitalizes on its pricing strategy, which continues to drive occupancy. Like Hilton (HLT), MAR sets competitive pricing. In most of its geographical locations, occupancy and RevPAR inched up. Another factor was the disinflation in Q1. After the sudden inflation uptick from October to January, the Fed tamed it in the next two months, closing Q1 2025 at only 2.4%. The continued drop in oil prices also drove travel demand as they stabilized prices across industries. This was more impactful on transportation and fuel expenses.

More interestingly, MAR enhanced its efficiency, which I think should no longer surprise us. After all, it concentrates mainly on franchised and managed hotels. Its total operating costs and expenses rose by 4.2%. This was lower than revenue growth, showing MAR’s prudent management amid its expansion. Its operating margin improved from 14.5% to 15.1%. Even if we disregard the impact of cost reimbursements, the margin will still increase.

OPEC Oil Hike Can Support Travel Spending

New tariffs by Trump continue to rouse concerns across the globe. Yet, the decreasing Brent and WTI prices may partially offset its unfavorable impact. Currently, they are at their 52-week low, and since many oil producers pair their prices with these benchmarks, prices in other countries may follow. This can be sustained by the OPEC oil supply. Considering its influence and portion of the global inventory, this move can pull the price down further. In my view, global oil prices may even decrease further to $50 per barrel. As a result, many industries may lower their fuel-related expenses. This can help industries that heavily rely on energy, such as airlines, logistics, shipping, and restaurants. Hence, travelers may incur lower transportation expenses.

High Concentration On Franchised And Managed Hotels To Ensure Profitability and Sustainability

Most of MAR’s hotels and other accommodations are either franchised or managed. These are under the fee-based business segment of the company. I believe this helps the company maintain an advantage over many peers, particularly those with many owned and leased hotels in their portfolio.

Considering this, MAR can effectively manage its cost sensitivity and capital intensity. This makes it easy for MAR to set a competitive pricing strategy depending on market conditions. In addition, it generates revenues consistently from its franchisees and managed hotels. One way is by setting a specific portion or percentage of revenue from its franchised and managed hotels.

Expanding its market presence is also cheaper and easier for the company since it doesn’t have to spend billions on building its own properties. As of Q1, it already has 1,718,542 rooms in its over 9,000 properties. Only 0.8% of these are owned and leased by MAR. So, it should not surprise us that its number of rooms has already increased by about 44,000 from my previous coverage.

This increase is higher than HLT with only 30,000. And I think this has something to do with the fact that MAR has a lower number and portion of owned and leased hotels and rooms. If we average its reported revenue by its total number of rooms, MAR generates $3,644 versus HLT with $2,102.

External Laggards

The implementation of new tariffs is one of the things I expect to unfavorably impact MAR’s performance. While it does not have a direct impact on hotel expenses, there can be negative spillovers MAR should watch out for. Tariffs can be inflationary, which can affect revenues and earnings across industries. This can lower consumer confidence and spending. Worse, companies with a weaker cushion against tariffs may have to retrench employees, which can also lower travel demand.

In response, the Fed may either keep the interest rate unchanged or reverse its policy easing cycle to temper inflation. It is important to note that the majority of American travelers plan to use credit cards for their travel expenses. However, a substantial portion of travelers who used credit cards last summer 2024 remain unpaid.

Considering this, there may be weaker travel trends in the second half of 2025. In fact, Americans who plan to do trips this summer fell to 46%. Affordability is the main concern of travelers.

Balance Sheet Is Still Well-Positioned

One thing I failed to notice in my previous coverage is that borrowings have continuously risen and dramatically risen in the past 11 quarters. This appears too much considering the current cash levels. While we can attribute it to MAR’s continued expansion, we must beware of overleveraging in the long run.

On a lighter note, I believe MAR is still well-positioned. Its Net Debt/EBITDA remains decent at 3.3x, which is still lower than the maximum ideal ratio of 3.5-4.0x. This is one aspect that makes MAR better than Hilton. The company only needs about three years to pay all its borrowings. It also means the company is earning enough to cover its repayments. In addition, 49% of its borrowings have maturities of more than five years.

Price Ratio

In my previous coverage, all I did was compare the valuation of MAR to other big hotel stocks like HLT and Hyatt (H). However, I failed to assess how its price has really changed along with key financial metrics over the years. Looking at its EPS, Sales Per Share (SPS), Cash Per Share (CPS), and FCF Per Share (FCFS), I noticed that the stock valuation is a bit too stretched compared to previous years. Take EPS as an example. The stock trades at about 29x its EPS, which is still quite higher than the five-year average. This gives a TP of $255.84, justifying the recent downtrend. Note that I smoothed out the 2020 EPS to remove the negative value. The other financial metrics also show that the stock is still a bit too high for its current valuation.

To determine price sensitivity to them, I also did a regression analysis. True enough, I computed a 95% correlation. This shows that when they increase or decrease, there is a 95% possibility that the price will follow their trend. However, only the EPS and FCFS have a p-value of less than 0.05. This is crucial because a higher p-value means higher data randomness. These two have a p-value of 0.03 and 0.02, respectively. They show that less than 5% of their relationship with the stock price happened by chance. They prove the consistency of their direct correlation. While we used historical values, these are enough evidence to help us predict the potential price direction. Hence, it is safe to say that the stock is still quite high now.

Technicals

MAR’s momentum is still weak today, and sustaining its rebound can still be challenging. We must note that the 50-day SMA is now moving below the 200-day SMA following the Death Cross formation. Although the price seems to be moving above both SMAs, we can notice its weakening momentum. In addition, the 200-day SMA has already flattened, showing that both short and long-run sentiments remain pessimistic.

This can be confirmed by MACD and RSI. Both are increasing from the oversold region. They show that many buyers found support when the price landed at $208. However, we can note that the RSI is nearing the overbought region. MACD histogram also indicates weakening buying volume. I think that’s logical if we match it with the Fibonacci Retracement. The stock is testing the 0.50 retracement point at $256.20, so should consider the possibility of it retracing to the 0.382 retracement point. If it hits it, there may be a chance of uptrend continuation after the retracement. Hence, I advise investors to wait for now to assess the market behavior.

Conclusion

Marriott International, Inc. remains a solid hotel stock in my watchlist, much better than HLT and H. Its well-balanced topline growth and secured Balance Sheet show profitability and sustainability. Yet, the current price is still quite high to make a position. Technicals also show some risks, so lightening up MAR’s shares can be wise to minimize risks without removing it completely. I am giving this stock a hold rating for now.

data.title: Capital Southwest: Stronger Than You Think, The Good And The Bad
data.description:
Capital Southwest (NASDAQ:CSWC) is one of the most interesting picks in the current market envirionment, despite the negative sentiment it's faced since mid 2024.

During the last year, its stock price decreased by nearly 25%. I've already considered CSWC a buying opportunity in December 2024, but its stock price fell even harder as a reaction to Trump's announcement of tariffs. Are there reasonable factors behind the sell-off? Yes and I'll discuss them. Is CSWC an attractive investment opportunity? I think it is, and I'll show you why.

The Bad: Trump's Tariffs Will Put Pressure On Already Challenged Portfolio Quality

CSWC already has its non-accruals elevated. At year-end, they amounted to 2.7% at FV and 5.2% at cost vs 2.3% and 3.9%, respectively, at the end of March 2025. Both metrics increased and many quality BDCs like Ares Capital (ARCC), Sixth Street Capital (TSLX), or even safer players like Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL) or Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (MSDL) have much more reasonable non-accrual levels, often not-exceeding 1%. That's crucial for BDC's performance as a capital provider, especially debt capital, as investments are recognized as non-accruals when CSWC loses conviction of its ability to service its obligations.

That's why, at first sight, Trump's tariffs may prove even more harmful to CSWC given its elevated non-accruals. There's a likelihood of economic downturn, inflationary pressure, increased import costs, and thus - reduced consumer and B2B spending. I have to note that since interest rates started to increase in 2022, businesses have operated under a high interest rate environment for quite some time. That leads to a conclusion that many can already be struggling with servicing obligations - especially those operating on the lower-end of the market. Such drastic changes to global trade could increase these challenges and at the same time - increase the risk of non-accruals.

With all that we should also take a look at CSWC's debt obligations, but the good news is that its nearest debt maturity will happen in October 2026, leaving some time for interest rates to decrease (potentially).

So... It seems that the economic environment became much harder and businesses will face quite a lot of uncertainty in the upcoming quarters. What's worth to highlight here is that CSWC focuses on lower middle-market companies, which will likely be heavier impacted by economic downturn (as it usually goes - the larger you are, the higher probability you have to survive tough times). The average EBITDA of CSWC's portfolio companies amounts to just $20m. I say "just", as there are several BDCs focusing on higher market segments with averages and medians in the $100-$200m range.

The Good: CSWC Is Stronger Than It Seems

I've talked about non-accruals a bit and as stated - I consider the risk of their increase in the upcoming quarters higher than I used to. While CSWC's non-accruals are relatively elevated, there's something investors miss when looking solely at non-accruals. Despite the increase in non-performing investments, the quality of CSWC's portfolio actually improved in 2024 - especially at its higher-end.

Take a look at CSWC's internal performance ratings - especially at numbers 1 and 2. Number 1 stands for investments performing materially above expectations and number 2 stands for investments performing according to expectations. CSWC substantially increased the share of 1-rated investments since March 2024 to December 2024 - it grew from 14.7% to 26.9%. Naturally the shift happened between number 2 and 1, but positive changes materially outweigh negative ones in my taste (3-4-rated investments actually decreased by 0.1 percentage points).

However, non-accruals can still increase. They can still pose a threat to CSWC's performance. So... what's the tool for that? First-lien debt, as it ensured priority in the repayment ladder in the case of potential default of a portfolio company. It's also helpful with asset claims. CSWC's exposure to secured debt amounts to ~90.6% with 89.1% first-lien debt exposure. Naturally, there are players (like BXSL or MSDL) with substantially higher first-lien debt exposure (96-98%), but CSWC's position remains solid. The rest of the portfolio is allocated across equity positions, which should perform better in the case of further interest rate cuts.

Speaking of interest rates, further reductions will likely harm CSWC's dividend coverage (at least in the short-term), but I'll advocate for their positive impact on the BDC sector in the light of potential economic downturn. Lower interest rates will allow businesses to catch a breath in terms of liquidity or increase their eagerness to invest. On top of that, it will somewhat offset reduced B2B and B2C spending, as more money will be left in our pockets. Improved credit quality of the portfolio, which is a natural result of lower interest rates, would reduce the risk of non-accruals, so to all those afraid of interest rate decreases - I'm not afraid. I believe many BDCs, including CSWC, will benefit.

On top of that, CSWC is an internally managed BDC. One of the other internally managed BDCs - MAIN Street Capital (MAIN) summarized the benefits of that greatly:

Because we are internally managed, we do not pay any external investment advisory fees, but instead directly incur the operating costs associated with employing investment and portfolio management professionals. We believe that our internally managed structure provides us with a better alignment of interests between our management team and our employees and our shareholders and a beneficial operating expense structure when compared to other publicly traded and privately held investment firms which are externally managed, and our internally managed structure allows us the opportunity to leverage our noninterest operating expenses as we grow...

Upcoming Earnings Release - Preview

CSWC will announce its final Q4 2025 results next week - on May 15, 2025. We've already received some estimates (here's the link) and there are several positive news. Naturally, it's still subject to certain adjustments we will be able to review next week, but assuming no major deviations from CSWC's estimations, its shareholders may feel satisfied. Firstly, its net asset value is supposed to range from $16.65 to $16.75, indicating a growth of $0.06 to $0.16 since year-end 2024. While adjusted pre-tax NII is expected to range from $0.60-0.61 per share (a decrease vs previous quarters when it ranged from $0.64 to $0.68 per share), but it's quite reasonable given interest rate cuts, at the end of lat year - impacting future results.

What's most important to me is the major change in non-accruals at fair value, which are estimated at 1.7%. That will be something I will examine in detail after earnings release.

So... what should you check first when CSWC releases earnings? I will go straight into comments regarding non-accruals changes and internal performance ratings to see whether tendencies I mentioned earlier still hold true.

Therefore, CSWC May Prove To Be An Attractive Pick, Even With Market Struggles Ahead

Starting with $17.68 NAV in 2015, CSWC almost doubled that value since then through its $16.59 NAV and over $18 per share cumulative distributions. I believe its value proposition is much stronger than it used to be. Portfolio quality remains reasonable and non-accruals (even upcoming ones) should be manageable. On top of that, over the course of last year the premium to its NAV decreased from over 60% to ~20%, which I consider a fair price for the internally managed, first-lien oriented, and time-tested BDC.

On top of that, it pays reliable base dividends (currently $0.58 per share quarterly) with some supplemental payments along the way. The combination of these factors should ensure attractive risk-to-reward profile for CSWC's new shareholders.

With all factors I discussed in mind, both negative and positive, I uphold my "buy" rating for CSWC. I'm also looking forward to its upcoming earnigns release next week. For those hesitant, it may be worth waiting for their release. Cheers!

data.title: Franklin Biotechnology Discovery Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: After starting 2025 on a strong note, global equities collectively declined during the rest of the first quarter (1Q25) due to investor concerns about US economic growth, President Donald Trump's trade policy and a broadening trade war. Despite the overall weakness, eight out of 11 global equity sectors still managed to advance, including a fifth place finish for health care. The sector's better-than-average result was driven by several key factors, including (1) interesting valuations after underperforming in 2024; (2) continued advancements in medical technology and new drug approvals; and (3) strong fourth-quarter 2024 earnings reports from major health care companies.
  • Detractors: Stock selection in the biotechnology industry and a small, off-benchmark exposure to the managed health care industry.
  • Contributors: An overweight in the health care services industry, positioning in the pharmaceuticals industry, and stock selection and an underweight in the life sciences tools and services industry.
  • Outlook: We are carefully monitoring emerging 2025 risks related to global tariffs, health care regulation and US Food & Drug Administration (FDA) staffing. We also remain cognizant of risks to biopharma innovation and stock performance linked to persistent inflation and the potential for only minimal interest-rate cuts in the United States in 2025.

Performance Review

  • Most of the fund's performance gap versus the benchmark NASDAQ Biotechnology Index (XNBI) was due to unfavorable stock selection in the biotechnology industry, which comprised 82.28% of the portfolio at March-end. Overweighted or off-index positions in Vaxcyte (PCVX), UniQure (QURE), Praxis Precision Medicines (PRAX), Day One Biopharmaceuticals (DAWN), Jasper Therapeutics (JSPR), Crinetics Pharmaceuticals (CRNX), Dyne Therapeutics (DYN) and several other biotech detractors sold off sharply and saw their equity values reduced by more than 30%, which in most cases was due to clinical trial setbacks.
  • In general, the new US administration's unconventional appointments to key health care posts raised concerns about the direction of US biotech regulation. This uncertainty generally soured investor sentiment toward US-based biotech companies. The persistently high interest-rate environment in the United States has also been a disadvantage for domestic biotech companies, which typically have long drug development cycles. This has made it more challenging to secure funding and maintain profitability. The complex impact of the Inflation Reduction Act on drug pricing has added to the uncertainty. Global market dynamics were also at play: non-US biotech companies, particularly those in Europe and Asia, have benefited from more stable regulatory environments and favorable or improving economic conditions. Additionally, these regions have seen significant private and public funding rounds, which have bolstered investor confidence.
  • Although pharmaceuticals holdings ( 11.15% of the portfolio at March-end) added to both relative and absolute returns, there were several detractors that shed considerable value, including off-index Contineum Therapeutics (CTNM), which faced delays in its Phase 2 trial for PIPE-307, a treatment for relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis.
  • Among contributors, pharmaceuticals industry results were driven by a stellar gain for Intra-Cellular Therapies (not held at quarter-end) and a rally in Eli Lilly (LLY, not an index component). Johnson & Johnson (JNJ)not a fund holding) agreed to acquire all outstanding shares of Intra-Cellular for $132 per share in cash, totaling about $14.6 billion.
  • In biotech, the aforementioned relative-return setbacks were diminished by a host of notable contributors that included robust rallies in Ascendis Pharma (ASND), PTC Therapeutics (PTCT) and Mineralys Therapeutics (MLYS, all overweighted). The fund was also aided by a lighter-than-index exposure to Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (REGN) as its share price declined, and our avoidance of several index component companies that sold off.
  • In the health care services industry, an overweighted investment in Guardant Health (GH) appreciated solidly. Guardant, which is seeing exceptional demand for its products, focuses on cancer treatment through advanced diagnostics. Elsewhere in the portfolio, relative returns in the life sciences tools and services industry were aided foremost by our limited exposure to Illumina's (ILMN) steep selloff as we liquidated the position ahead of the worst of it.

Outlook

  • Recent developments related to FDA staffing and global tariffs add to near-term uncertainty. We are closely monitoring the impact of recent senior departures at the FDA, as well as the agency's larger workforce reductions. Individual company exposure to tariffs will depend on whether the fees are paid on active pharmaceutical ingredients (mostly imported) or finished goods (as a lot of the final product manufacturing facilities are in the United States). We are also focused on transfer pricing and taxation, direct-to-consumer advertising, and most-favored nation pricing as these appear to be additional focal points for the Trump administration. Companies with existing commercial drug franchises tend to have less exposure to FDA risks, on average, but they also have higher exposure to tariff uncertainties at this time.
  • Health care sector merger-and-acquisition (M&A) activity is more or less on hold these days as businesses work through a period of heightened uncertainty tied to the FDA and tariffs. However, as clarity on regulations and trade policy returns, we think M&A could switch back to a market tailwind as capital constraints put pressure on smaller or early-stage companies and as intensifying drug reimbursement pressures and patent-exclusivity losses impact larger commercial enterprises.
  • Alongside the biotech and pharma spheres, we are encouraged by what we are seeing in background processes, as novel discovery tools and the adoption of artificial intelligence and machine learning (ML) technologies are enabling faster and more rational drug discovery and development. While still in the early stages, the adoption of Al/ML tools in drug discovery is expected to grow rapidly in the near term. We believe AI/ML offers the potential to identify novel targets that were previously thought to be "undruggable," as well as improve drug design by simulating molecular behavior and interactions.

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the figures shown. Principal value and investment returns will fluctuate, and investors'shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Performance would have been lower iffees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit Mutual Funds | ETFs | Insights.

The fund began offering Advisor Class shares on 9/1/2009. Performance quotations have been calculated as follows: (A) for Advisor Class periods prior to 9/1/2009, a restated figure is used based on the fund's Class A performance. The performance was adjusted to take into account differences in class-specific operating expenses and maximum sales charges. (b) For periods after share class offering, performance for the specific share class is used, reflecting the expenses and maximum sales charges applicable to that class.

Gross expenses are the fund's total annual operating expenses as of the fund's prospectus available at the time of publication. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns. Net expenses reflect contractual fee waivers, expense caps and/or reimbursements, which cannot be terminated prior to 08/31/2025 without Board consent. Additional amounts may be voluntarily waived and/or reimbursed and may be modified or discontinued at any time without notice.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. The portfolio is non-diversified and may invest in a relatively small number of issuers, which may negatively impact the fund's performance and result in greater fluctuation in the value of the fund's shares. To the extent the portfolio invests in a concentration of certain securities, regions or industries, it is subject to increased volatility. Small- and mid-cap stocks involve greater risks and volatility than large-cap stocks. International investments are subject to special risks, including currency fluctuations and social, economic and political uncertainties, which could increase volatility. These risks are magnified in emerging markets. The manager may consider environmental, social and governance criteria in the research or investment process; however, ESG considerations may not be a determinative factor in security selection. In addition, the manager may not assess every investment for ESG criteria, and not every ESG factor may be identified or evaluated. These and other risks are discussed in the fund's prospectus.

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager's assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund's portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.The NASDAQ Biotechnology Index is a modified capitalization-weighted index designed to measure performance of all NASDAQ stocks in the biotechnology sector. data.title: Fund Flows In Focus Amid Market Volatility
data.description:
Trade and economic uncertainty continues to fuel market volatility and is prompting investors to look beyond the traditionally popular U.S. exchange-traded funds. Andres Rincon, Managing Director and Head of ETF Sales and Strategy with TD Securities joins MoneyTalk to discuss how investors are looking to diversify their ETF holdings.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: Uncertainty around trade and the economy has driven market volatility recently. But how are investors positioning themselves in this environment? Joining us now to discuss is Andres Rincon, Managing Director and Head of ETF Sales and Strategy with TD Securities.

Andres, always great to have you on the program.

Andres Rincon: Thank you for having me.

Greg Bonnell: April was quite the month. It's amazing when you take a look at a one-month chart of the S&P 500, where we were before the tariff announcement of April 2 and where we ended up. Quite a roller-coaster ride.

Andres Rincon: That's right.

Greg Bonnell: The buy the dip mentality, did that show up? Because it would have been advantageous for some.

Andres Rincon: Yeah. Interestingly, you would have thought that everybody would just went to cash, basically, given all the volatility and uncertainty. But we're actually seeing, still, a lot of activity in equities. I would say, compared to last year, though, quite more modest and reduced, just to be clear.

So there was some buying of the dip. We did see, still, interest in US equities. So call it, let's say, 40% of the flows into equity ETFs was in US equities. But that was much stronger last year. So I wanted to put that into context, because it's important. A lot more money today is going into international equities, European equities, European defense, which I think we talked about last time.

So there's a lot more interest in the UK, Europe in general today than there was, say, last year. Also, EM equities, in general, is seeing some momentum there. So all in all, you saw about-- just in Canada, for example, you saw $8 billion go into, call it, in the month of April, you saw go into US equities but 5.3 into international equities-- just to give you a perspective. And that number was very well skewed towards US equities.

So there's clearly some momentum and change in that space. In terms of equity ETFs, you saw a lot more interest in low-vol. So there is definitely some interest in more a safe haven equity world, but also a lot of money still going into fixed income.

So, the big transition from last year to this year is, we've seen a change in allocation more to fixed income versus last year, which was very much an equity-heavy year, obviously pre-Trump, let's say. This year, we've seen a lot more money go into fixed income.

So, what's really interesting is that the direct investing channel that-- your channel that we're talking to here, has a keen interest in money market ETFs, a little bit more so than high interest savings ETFs. Money market ETFs are paying a little bit more than high interest savings ETFs. So there's a lot more interest there.

On the advisor side, we're seeing, still, a lot more interest on your traditional fixed income, ag and whatnot. But money market is hitting it out of the park in our DI channel by magnitudes of numbers. Flows into money markets is much larger than equity and fixed income ETFs.

Greg Bonnell: Really? Wow. So it does give you a picture of the mentality of everyone, because these are uncertain times. When we think about ETFs, when we about key people moving to the US market through ETFs in Europe, I often think of a big basket of something that's tracking an index. Single-stock ETFs-- are people warming up to those?

Andres Rincon: 100%. They've become very popular in both US and Canada. These products are bought for a variety of reasons. So, obviously, people might ask you, why do you buy a single-stock ETF where you--

Greg Bonnell: Because they have the single stock.

Andres Rincon: You can just buy a single stock. But in reality, they offer a different outcome. So, what is generally offered in the ETF vehicle is a leveraged version of that stock and/or a leveraged yield version of that stock.

So, that's what's really popular. And actually, most of the flows go into the yielding version of that stock. What's really popular, for example, is there's some tech stocks, let's say, that are not really high dividend-paying stocks. So, some of these ETFs offer a yielding version of those. And some people find that attractive, so they're seeing a lot of flows there.

I would say, 2/3rd of the assets going into single stock ETFs go into the yielding version versus just the leveraged version of that ETF. But they're seeing incredible growth, especially in the really, call it, popular names on the street, like a MicroStrategy (MSTR), like a Tesla (TSLA), like an Apple (AAPL). Those names are very popular in ETF form.

Greg Bonnell: What you need to be aware of, though, if you're playing in that space? If you're taking a look at that, say, hey, there's a yield here, let's go there.

Andres Rincon: Oh, there's many things that you have to be aware of. For starters, a lot of the leveraged products-- the leverage varies wildly, from just one quarter turns of 0.25% of leverage, or 25% leverage, let's say, to two times, three times in some cases, depending on if it's US or Canada. And Canada, they just go to 25% extra leverage.

So it's relatively modest in Canada. So that means it's just going to add a little bit more volatility and/or yield to that position. In the US, they can go to two times, let's say, or higher. So, volatility is much higher.

When you have a leveraged product like that, they provide you with daily leverage, not long-term leverage. And that means that the outcome over a long period of time is very different than what you might think if you're buying a product that's two times. You're not getting long-term two times exposure, you're getting daily two times exposure. Which means that the volatility of that stock heavily impacts the return of that ETF. And it might not be what a lot of people think.

So I do encourage everybody to do their education with the leverage products. They're quite tricky in understanding them. And they're meant as trading vehicles, not really investing vehicles, for the two times. Now, for the 25% ones, slightly different story there.

Original Post

data.title: Greenwich LifeSciences: What The Recent Data Do For The Investment Thesis
data.description:
Topline Summary and Update

Greenwich LifeSciences (GLSI) is a company focused on developing novel treatments for HER2-positive breast cancer, and I have carried a relatively negative sentiment for the investment thesis over the articles I've written. Recently, the stock price has rallied off a bottom based on the announcement of new trial data. Today, let's take another look to see how their finances are shaping up, and whether this new announcement moves the needle.

Pipeline Updates

GLSI-100

The company's main and only pipeline candidate remains GLSI-100, a vaccine designed to stimulate an immune response against a peptide called GP2, which is made up of 9 amino acids from the transmembrane domain of HER2. In theory, this should trigger T cell production to hunt and destroy cells that overexpress the HER2 protein. This peptide was chosen for exploration in HER2-positive breast cancer due to its observed immunogenicity in preclinical models.

In the last article, I covered the initiation of the phase 3 FLAMINGO-01 study, which focuses on patients with residual cancer following neoadjuvant treatment and surgery. The study's recruitment of trial sites has continued at a rapid clip.

A month ago, however, GLSI announced some preliminary findings from FLAMINGO-01. Specifically, patients who are receiving GLSI-100 appear to be having more frequent injection site reactions and delayed-type sensitivity skin test reaction as they receive subsequent doses of the vaccine. Moreover, they are observing evidence that patients, regardless of HLA-A*02 status, are having immune reactions to GP2.

These findings imply that on some level the immune system is mounting a response to the GP2 peptide. In the announcement, the company assured that the study remains blinded, and that these open-label immunogenicity data were conducted in a way to preserve blinding, an important component of a would-be pivotal trial to prevent various biases.

GLSI also noted that these immunogenicity findings are consistent with data from a prior phase 2b study, which had appeared to show a substantial reduction in the risk of recurrence among strongly HER2-positive breast cancer patients (i.e., those with HER2 IHC 3+, which is the highest category of HER2 recognized).

Shortly after this announcement, GLSI provided a global update on the FLAMINGO-01 study. Notably, the company is considering expanding the non-HLA-A*02 open-label portion into essentially a second phase 3 trial, rather than one arm of the FLAMINGO-01 study. This could open up a wider label, should the data suffice to pursue approval. GLSI also intends to activate 30 more sites in the EU.

Financial Overview

At the end of 2024, GLSI held $4.1 million in cash, while incurring $16.0 million in operating expenses. The net loss for the year was $15.8 million, with cash burn of $7.3 million. This cash burn was offset through the sale of common stock via an ATM program and a private placement. They also noted in the 10K that they have sold $1.2 million worth of shares in the ATM arrangement between January 1 and April 11, 2025.

At this cash burn rate, GLSI currently has funding on hand to sustain around 2 to 3 quarters of operations, assuming no other financing arrangements are identified.

Strengths and Risks

Strength - A late-stage candidate puts possible approval on the horizon

Being deep in the middle of recruitment for their phase 3 trial, GLSI is successfully pushing this boulder up the hill. And their preliminary immunogenicity update here does not bode poorly for their approach. And treatment is apparently well tolerated so far.

Of course, it doesn't say too much about what we'll see with respect to efficacy, in the end. Top-line data is going to be critical, and I have not seen management give guidance on when these data is going to become available. The clinicaltrials.gov entry has an estimated primary completion date of December 2026.

Risk - HER2-positive breast cancer is a rapidly evolving field that may leave GLSI-100 behind, even if it works

Unfortunately, the unmet needs in HER2-positive breast cancer are continuing to evolve. In particular, Daiichi Sankyo's Enhertu is taking almost every treatment setting of HER2-positive breast cancer by storm, the most recent being the announcement of positive data from the DESTINY-Breast11 study.

This showed that adding Enhertu into the neoadjuvant therapy approach substantially improved the rates of patients achieving complete elimination of viable tumor after surgery. If approved, this poses 2 issues for GLSI. First, FLAMINGO-01's population does not include those who were previously treated with Enhertu, so the treatment decision-making would be complicated.

Second, if patients can achieve pathologic complete response by adding Enhertu, then they by definition would not fit the criteria for the FLAMINGO-01 study (i.e., needing to have residual disease). For many patients, the niche here would be eliminated, therefore shrinking the potential market size, which was already limited to just those patients with the strongest level of HER2 overexpression.

Risk - Cash is in a critical condition

GLSI has continued to operate on a shoestring budget through creative financing arrangements. Unfortunately, without some kind of definitive addressing of their cash situation, would-be shareholders are being subjected to nearly invisible dilution because of the ATM arrangement. This represents a major drag for the company's thesis if and until it is addressed.

Bottom-Line Summary

Wasting no time for this, GLSI continues to be a challenging investment thesis to consider. As I stated before, it's already tough to move the needle in early-stage HER2-positive breast cancer. Now they're trying to do it with a rapidly improving standard of care, and they're trying to do it with next to no financial resources to back it up.

At a market cap of just $125 million at the time of writing, clearly the market is viewing GLSI at a premium relative to their assets. This valuation, considered against the risks to their finances and their clinical program, appears to be overvalued, in my opinion.

With numerous downside risks in play, I continue to hold a negative, "Sell" sentiment on the equity at this time. It will be worth watching to see how it continues to develop, but I think the risk of a declining investment is too high.

data.title: Franklin Moderate Allocation Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • After starting 2025 on a strong note, global equities collectively declined in local-currency terms during the rest of the first quarter due to investor concerns about US economic growth, President Donald Trump's trade policy and a broadening trade war. On the economic front, global manufacturing activity expanded in March for the third consecutive month, and flash reports for March showed that global services activity continued to grow in several regions during the quarter.
  • US stocks experienced heightened volatility during the first quarter amid concerns about Trump's tariff policies and their impact on economic growth, inflation and the US Federal Reserve's interest-rate path, with some investors fearing a potential recession or stagflation (stagnant economy and high inflation). Underwhelming earnings reports and guidance from some companies and significantly lower consumer confidence also hindered sentiment.
  • Global aggregate bond indexes were mixed in the first quarter in US dollar terms as sovereign bond yields rose in many countries outside of the United States. The yield on the benchmark 10-year US Treasury (US10Y) note moved 33 bps lower to 4.24% in the first quarter

Performance Review

  • Fund selection in equity strengthened relative performance, including benefical selection in US, international and emerging markets.
  • Within US equity, an underlying value oriented fund contributed during a strong quarter for value stocks relative to growth stocks.
  • Regional allocations in fixed income, notably a substantial US overweight and non-US underweight, lifted relative results.
  • In contrast, an overweight to equity over fixed income detracted, reflecting a cross asset tactical preference.
  • Regional allocations in equity weighed on returns, including a US overweight, along with underweight exposure to international and emerging markets.
  • Fund selection in US fixed income hindered performance, due in part to an underlying high-yield bond fund.

Outlook

  • The overhang of US policy uncertainty and introduction of disruptive tariffs is having a major impact on global trade and business confidence. From an economic standpoint, this sets up a stagflationary environment in the United States, with tariffs causing a slowdown in growth while stoking inflation expectations. Despite a relatively resilient labor market and corporate sector thus far, we believe the risks of an outright recession are rising.
  • With inflation risks rising again, central banks are likely to proceed with caution in easing monetary policy further, in our opinion. This restraint would leave less leeway to support the economy in the early stages of a downturn. We believe equity markets may exhibit further weakness in the medium term as earnings estimates and valuations do not adequately reflect these risks. However, we are prepared to take a longer-term view and are paying close attention to identify compelling buying opportunities.
  • As we entered April, a tariff announcement from the United States was front and center in the news. While the events are still unfolding at the time of this writing, we can share a few thoughts. Typically, our investment strategy is to avoid selling into fear. This is especially prudent given the potential for rapid changes to policy-a hallmark of Trump's approach thus far.
  • However, given the rapid and sizeable changes, our conviction has been lowered. Some of the economic and corporate signposts we are watching include: 1) growth data and corporate guidance, 2) demand elasticity as a reaction to potential price increases, 3) measures of sentiment and market volatility, and 4) positioning data and flows of systematic sellers. We continue to closely monitor developments.

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the figures shown. Principal value and investment returns will fluctuate, and investors'shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Performance would have been lower iffees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit Mutual Funds | ETFs | Insights.

Gross expenses are the fund's total annual operating expenses as of the fund's prospectus available at the time of publication. Net expenses are capped under a contractual agreement, which cannot be terminated prior to 04/30/2025 without Board consent. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. The allocation of assets among different strategies, asset classes and investments may not prove beneficial or produce the desired results. Investments in underlying funds are subject to the same risks as, and indirectly bear the fees and expenses of, the underlying funds. Equity securities are subject to price fluctuation and possible loss of principal. Small- and mid-cap stocks involve greater risks and volatility than large-cap stocks. Fixed income securities involve interest rate, credit, inflation and reinvestment risks, and possible loss of principal. As interest rates rise, the value of fixed income securities falls. These and other risks are discussed in the fund's prospectus.

Glossary

Stagflation is a seemingly contradictory condition described by slow economic growth and relatively high unemployment, or economic stagnation, which is at the same time accompanied by rising prices (i.e. inflation).

Important Information

Effective January 1, 2015, Franklin Moderate Allocation Fund increased target allocation to equity funds by 5% (from 55% to 60% ) and correspondingly decreased the target allocation to fixed income funds (from 45% to 40%). The fund's investment goal remains the same. On January 1, 2013, the fund changed its target allocation, with short-term investments (formerly a targeted allocation of 10% ) combined into the fixed income allocations; such a change can impact performance. The fund's investment goal remained the same. The risk/reward potential is based on the fund's goal and level of risk. It is not indicative of the fund's actual or implied performance or portfolio composition, which may change on a continuous basis.The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager's assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy, it is intended only to provide insight into the fund's portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.The Linked Franklin Moderate Allocation Fund Benchmark-NR reflects the performance of 38.5% S&P 500 Index, 16.5% MSCI EAFE Index, 35% Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index and 10% Payden & Rygel 90 Day U.S. T-Bill Index from fund inception to 12/31/2000; 38.5% S&P 500 Index, 16.5% MSCI EAFE Index-NR, 35% Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index and 10% Payden & Rygel 90 Day U.S. T-Bill Index from 1///2001 to 12/31/2012; 38.5% S&P 500 Index, 16.5% MSCI EAFE Index-NR and 45% Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index from 1/1/2013 to 12/31/14; and 60% MSCI AC World Index-NR and 40% Bloomberg Multiverse Index thereafter. data.title: First Solar Is A Political Football, But Also A Long-Term Winner
data.description:
First Solar (FSLR) disappointed investors with its latest 1Q25 earnings release. The company lowered its FY25 top and bottom-line projections due to ‘considerable challenges’ from the ongoing tit-for-tat trade war. FSLR has returned about -26% year-to-date, underperforming all relevant indices, including the solar energy-focused TAN ETF, potentially due to the uncertainty surrounding its tax credit benefits.

Investment Thesis

According to our estimates, FSLR is expected to receive about $19.64B in 45X tax credits through 2032. The stock's valuation hinges on receiving this amount over the next seven years, while the Trump administration is taking decisive steps to undermine it (more on this below).

Our DCF valuation suggests that the market discounts FSLR's tax credit income entirely, presenting a ~75% upside to optimistic long-term investors who believe that 45X benefits will remain. Although we believe a full funding freeze is unlikely, our weighted average target price accounts for potential delays or reductions.

Beyond the tax credits, First Solar's CdTe technology faces an inherent efficiency lag versus the Chinese-dominated crystalline silicon solar panels, but benefits from superior manufacturing processes and lower cost per watt.

Due to the current market pessimism, the negative tariff impact, and a slowing in bookings growth, weighing on the stock, we initiate FSLR with a 'HOLD' recommendation while looking to capitalize on any dips.

Finally, our analysis of First Solar's technology suggests a potential hidden beneficiary of FSLR's success, the biggest U.S. copper miner, Freeport-McMoRan (FCX).

Company Overview

First Solar is a vertically integrated, U.S.-based solar technology company that develops and manufactures advanced photovoltaic modules used to generate clean solar energy. It is the biggest domestic and only gigawatt-scale cadmium telluride (CdTe) solar panel manufacturer in the world and differentiates itself through its proprietary technology, which competes with the traditional crystalline silicon modules made overwhelmingly in China.

First Solar has a big manufacturing footprint in the United States with 11 GW of manufacturing capacity and operates factories in Malaysia and Vietnam (7.1 GW combined) and India (0.6 GW). Due to the ongoing trade war and tariffs imposed on all of those countries by the Trump administration, its international factories face challenges, with some of its production capacity coming offline. According to the company's latest 10-K report, FSLR expects "to have an annual manufacturing capacity of over 25 GW by 2026."

To put things into context, we first need to highlight that, contrary to other solar energy companies like SolarEdge (SEDG) and Enphase (ENPH), which sell directly to consumers, First Solar’s business focuses on utility-scale projects and does not sell directly to households.

FSLR’s primary revenue stream comes from solar panel sales to entities building utility-scale solar farms, including independent power producers (“IPPs”), commercial or industrial companies, and clean energy project developers. The high-volume, long-term supply contracts make the company less susceptible to, but not immune from, the industry’s highly cyclical nature.

Over the last few years, First Solar transitioned out of the Project Development business and no longer develops solar power plants. Although this segment is not active, some of the company’s revenues come from this legacy business as well as from the corresponding operations and maintenance (“O&M”) services for such projects.

Finally, FSLR offers Recycling Services for decommissioned photovoltaic modules. This business enhances the company’s ESG profile, but does not contribute meaningfully to its revenues.

Resilient Financial Performance

Since 2020, the company has grown its top line at a respectable 11.6% CAGR, including a temporary contraction in 2022 during the post-COVID demand slump.

As a quick note, the company faced several challenges in 2022, including divestitures, high production start-up costs, planned downtime in manufacturing facilities, lower average selling prices ("ASP"), and higher freight and logistics expenses.

The Inflation Reduction Act and the relevant 45X tax credits came into effect for all solar panels produced within the U.S. starting January 1st, 2023. First Solar received a net amount of $658 million and $819 million from 45X tax credits (sold for cash) in 2023 and 2024, respectively. The company treats these tax credits as a reduction in its Cost of Sales, artificially inflating its Gross Profit. Without the tax credits, FSLR's Gross Margin would have dropped from a reported 39.2% to 19.4% in 2023 and from 44.2% to 24.7% in 2024.

As we will see in the Valuation section, the presence and scale of the 45X tax credits are critical to FSLR's valuation.

Due to its aggressive expansion plans, the company is expending cash, as evidenced by the consistently negative Free Cash Flow over the last five years. At the same time, the company's investments translate into improving manufacturing capacity over the years.

Deteriorating Financial Position

First Solar's net debt position is deteriorating over time. In the latest 1Q25 quarter, net cash dropped to $366M, down from $1,074M in FY24. The increasing total assets underline the company's active manufacturing footprint expansion in the U.S.

At the same time, the company's receivables and inventories are increasing simultaneously, causing a sharp decline in Operating Cash Flow.

How Competitive Is FSLR’s Cadmium Telluride (CdTe) Technology?

According to a paper by the U.S. Department of Energy, CdTe accounted for about 34% of the US utility solar farm market as of 2022, and only 4% globally. Globally, over 1,000 GW of C-Si photovoltaics are installed, versus just 30 GW of CdTe photovoltaics.

That begs two questions: why is FSLR's Cadmium-Telluride (CdTe) technology lagging behind the Crystalline-Silicon (C-Si) in commercial installations, and why doesn't First Solar sell to homeowners? The answer to both questions is efficiency (i.e., how much solar radiation is converted into electricity).

The Drawbacks of CdTe

First Solar's technology is inherently less efficient than the competing Chinese-dominated C-Si technology. While CdTe achieves an ~18% efficiency under standard test conditions, C-Si yields approximately 22%. Due to the high volume, multi-decade nature of utility-scale photovoltaic parks, a 4% difference in efficiency leads to considerable long-term electricity losses.

Practically, FSLR-supplied photovoltaic parks must have up to 31% greater land coverage to produce the same energy as a comparative crystalline silicon panel project over their lifetime. This makes area-constrained applications (e.g., rooftop installations) much less competitive for First Solar, which explains why the company does not sell to individual homeowners.

Also, tellurium, a core metalloid used to produce CdTe panels, is four times rarer than gold and is primarily manufactured as a byproduct of copper refining. That is contrary to silicon, which is the second most abundant mineral found in the Earth's crust. Finally, copper doping, a method used to improve CdTe solar panel efficiency to competitive levels, reduces the module's life expectancy considerably.

On the other hand, some key benefits of FSLR's solar panel technology, particularly in its manufacturing process, make it a viable alternative in large-scale solar farms.

Positives

CdTe's benefits come from its superior manufacturing efficiency and cost. Its overall cost is cheaper, ranging from $0.20 to $0.35 per watt, versus silicon panels at $0.30 to $0.70. This is partly due to much thinner active layers versus the silicon comparatives (1-6 microns vs. 180 microns for silicon).

It has a much faster energy payback period (energy used to produce the panel Vs energy produced by the panel), has superior durability, holding about 89% of its production capacity over 30 years. First Solar's units have a much faster production time (4.5 hours per panel) and are more environmentally friendly with a lower carbon and water footprint. Finally, CdTe units outperform their C-Si counterparts in high-temperature and humid environments.

Bottom Line For First Solar's CdTe Technology

As things stand, First Solar's CdTe panels are a secondary choice to C-Si panels for large-scale commercial solar farm projects due to a lower field efficiency and greater land use requirements. For the company's technology to remain competitive and gain market share, it is important to monitor three key factors.

  1. Tariffs on the Chinese-dominated crystalline silicon ("C-Si") solar panels.
  2. Incentives for U.S.-produced cadmium telluride ("CdTe") solar panels.
  3. Innovation and efficiency gains in CdTe technology over time.

In the medium term, we don't see meaningful export volumes for First Solar until the technology evolves into a worthy alternative from a Levelized Cost of Electricity ("LCOE") perspective.

Bonus Insight - FCX Could Benefit

As mentioned, CdTe supply is constrained by tellurium, a rare element, about four times more scarce than gold. It is produced as a byproduct of copper refining.

Freeport-McMoRan, the biggest domestic copper miner in the United States, could be an indirect beneficiary of FSLR's success. Currently, FCX's Texas refinery produces copper telluride, although the company does not refine it.

China, which dominates the refined tellurium supply worldwide, imposed export restrictions in February 2025. With this development, FCX could enter the copper telluride refining business, guaranteeing a domestic supplier for FSLR as it ramps up its production.

Please let us know in the comments whether we should cover FCX and the potential impact of a copper telluride refining business next.

Valuation

Quantifying The Tax Credit Impact

First Solar's valuation hinges on the presence and scale of the Advanced Manufacturing Production Credits ("45X"). These were introduced under the Biden administration through the Inflation Reduction Act in 2022 to boost domestic manufacturing of clean energy components. It came into effect as of 2023 for all eligible components produced in the U.S. and was initially planned to last until 2030 in full and then phase out by 25% per year until 2032.

Table 1: First Solar's annual solar panel production, sales, production capacity, and net tax credit proceeds after selling for cash.

Year Annual Production (GW) Sold (GW) Production Capacity (GW) 45X Net Proceeds ($ million) $ Cents per watt
FY25 (exp.) 18.0 - 19.0 exp. 18-20 exp. 25.0 exp. 1,650 - 1,700 0.089
FY24 15.5 14.1 21 819 0.053
FY23 12.1 11.4 16.6 660 0.055
FY22 9.1 - 9.8 - -

We use the information provided above to model the proceeds. For annual production, we assume mid-guidance numbers for 2025, a 15% growth for 2026, and a 10% growth thereafter.

For the 45X Net Proceeds, we assume the company will continue to increase its domestic (U.S.) output, increasing its 45X proceeds per watt at mid-guidance in 2025 and 5% thereafter, converging to the company's stated $17c per watt.

Using a flat discount rate of 20% to account for the uncertainty of the tax credits' longevity, timing, and presence under the hostile Trump administration, we estimate the present value of the remaining 45X cash flows throughout for FSLR to be ~$10.54B.

Valuing FSLR With and Without Tax Credits

Our Free Cash Flow to the Firm ("FCFF") valuation uses Bloomberg estimates ("BEst") for 2025-2029. We assume a flat 5% growth rate thereafter and a 4% terminal growth rate. The discount rate, WACC, is set at 11%, aligning with the company's latest capital structure and costs.

We reach a Target Price of $220 (+76% upside from $125) for FSLR, 45% of which is attributed to the 45X tax credits. Assuming the tax credits vanish completely going forward, we reach a TP of $121 (-3% from $125).

We believe that a complete cut of the 45X tax credit is unlikely. Instead, reduction or distribution delays are more likely. By equal weighting the two scenarios to account for this uncertainty, we reach a Blended Target Price of $171, demonstrating a +36% upside potential (from $125).

Action Recommendation

While we see a meaningful upside to the stock, reflected by our view that complete tax credit cuts are unlikely, some factors lead us to initiate FSLR with a 'Hold' recommendation.

First, the current market pessimism is weighing on renewable energy stocks with an unfavorable U.S. administration. Second, the negative tariff impact, which might persist, affects First Solar's international plants. Finally, a slowing in bookings growth combined with a growing capacity.

We are looking to capitalize on meaningful stock price weaknesses around the $100 level.

When To Turn More Bullish

To turn more bullish, we would like to see FSLR benefit from the AI infrastructure buildup through strong bookings growth, clarity around the trade war impact and the 45X tax credits, breakthroughs in CdTe technology, and/or further tariffs on the competing crystalline silicon solar panels.

Risks

Since day one, the Trump administration has taken decisive steps to undermine the Inflation Reduction Act, which benefits FSLR through the 45X tax credits. While lawsuits challenged Trump's ordered funding freeze, the current administration continues to undermine the IRA indirectly by rolling back energy efficiency standards and issuing executive orders to preempt state-level clean energy regulations. As demonstrated in the "Valuation" section, cutting the 45X tax credits could cut the company's valuation in half.

First Solar's cadmium telluride ("CdTe") technology lags behind its silicon counterparts due to its inherent efficiency lag in real-world applications. In order to remain competitive in the long run, the company must spend significant amounts on R&D to improve its technology and gain market share.

The company's technology relies on critical metals such as telluride, which now faces export controls from its dominant producer, China. The current trade tensions between the U.S. and China could limit FSLR's output capacity and/or increase its costs.

Although profitable in accounting terms, FSLR continues to spend significant cash as it ramps up production. Combined with a low bookings growth over the last quarters, the company could face a supply-demand imbalance if this trend continues.

Conclusion

About half of First Solar's fair market value hinges on the future of the 45X tax credits, which face pushback from the Trump administration. While we believe the incentives are unlikely to be cut completely, our target price of $171 (+36% from $125) adopts a conservative approach, equally weighting the two scenarios to account for potential delays and partial funding cuts. Optimistic investors who believe the 45X tax credits will remain intact should find FSLR attractive at current prices, with a target price of $220 (+76% from $125).

While boasting meaningful manufacturing and environmental advantages, FSLR's CdTe technology requires government incentives, tariffs on the competing C-Si panels, and/or field efficiency gains through R&D to compete and gain market share.

In the long term, First Solar's expanding U.S. presence and the country's manufacturing reshoring strategy, combined with an expected growing electricity demand and transition into green energy, make the stock's prospects appealing.

Due to the current market pessimism clouding renewable energy stocks, combined with an unfavorable and unpredictable U.S. administration, a negative tariff impact on the company's international capacity, and a slowing in bookings growth, FSLR could present a more attractive risk/reward soon. We initiate FSLR with a 'HOLD' recommendation while looking to capitalize on share price weakness around $100.

data.title: Elmos Semiconductor SE 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Elmos Semiconductor SE in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: Rivian: Near-Term Deliveries Cut Has No Bearing On R2's Future
data.description:
With a recession, either already underway or just around the corner, consumers have pulled back sharply on spending, particularly on big-ticket items like RVs. Tesla (TSLA) sales are declining, and even Rivian (NASDAQ:RIVN), despite not facing the Elon Musk unpopularity problem, is facing a slowdown.

Rivian just reported Q1 results, and though the company outperformed quite materially on the margin side, investors sent the stock downward on its guidance cut for the year. Still, I find that the slight reduction to current-year deliveries has very little impact on the company's longer-term prospects, especially when the company's R2 lineup is due for launch next year.

I last wrote a buy opinion on Rivian in early April, when the stock was still trading near $11 per share. Since then, Rivian has enjoyed a rebound rally alongside the rest of the stock market. In my view, I'm keen to ignore the short-term noise on deliveries downside and instead focus on A) the cost takeouts that Rivian is able to achieve in its existing vehicles, and B) the go-to-market progress that Rivian is making outside of its R1 lineup, which will be "old news" by next year anyway. I'm reiterating my buy rating here.

One of the compelling near-term drivers for the company is the fact that Rivian has continued to expand its customer base for its commercial van lineup. As a reminder, Rivian launched the electric vehicle delivery lineup for Amazon, and as of the end of last year, according to Amazon's own website, it has taken delivery of 20,000 of its total 100,000 vehicle orders.

In April, Rivian signed its first major partner outside of Amazon: HelloFresh, the German meal-kit company and one of the most notable tech startups in Europe. So far, HelloFresh has deployed 70 new Rivian commercial vans for its Factor subsidiary (a ready-to-eat meal service targeted toward busy, fitness-oriented professionals).

The deal in itself may not be large at the outset, but it dramatically expands Rivian's addressable market to other enterprise brands. On top of the market that Rivian will unlock next year through its $45,000 R2 model, Rivian is well poised for long-term growth.

Stay long here and use the dip as a near-term buying opportunity.

Q1 download: despite the guidance cut, focus on a massive revenue beat and aggressive margin progress

Let's now go through Rivian's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Rivian's revenue grew 3% y/y to $1.24 billion, substantially beating Wall Street's expectations of just $1.02 billion (-15% y/y) by an eighteen-point margin. Now, it must be noted here that Rivian, like all its other EV rivals, reports its production and delivery units a month ahead of its earnings release. Rivian had already reported its 8,640 delivery units back in April, which at the time slightly edged out over Street estimates of 8,610 vehicles.

So the revenue beat on already-known units means that there is tremendous upside on vehicle ASP. Calculated in-quarter ASP works out to $107k ($922 million in auto revenue, divided by 8,640 vehicles), which rose sharply versus $82k in the year-ago quarter: suggesting that though Rivian's deliveries shrunk versus last year, the buyers who have held on are gravitating toward the company's more expensive models. We note as well that software and services revenue more than tripled to $318 million, providing not only another revenue stream but a major benefit to the company's gross margins. This growth was enabled by advances in the company's Rivian Autonomy platform, which is now capable of autonomous freeway driving.

Note that we don't necessarily think the y/y decline in deliveries is a major red flag, but a normal pullback as Rivian prepares for a major R2 product transition next year. I also have the same sentiment when considering Rivian's outlook, which it slashed to 40-46k vehicles (-22% to -11% y/y), from a prior view of 46-51k deliveries (-11% y/y to -1% y/y).

To me, the cut in Rivian's outlook is counterbalanced by two things. The first, as previously mentioned, is massive outperformance on vehicle ASP, which clearly made up a lot of ground in Q1. The second related factor is that Rivian is making major advances in gross margin.

Gross margins in Q1 rose 7 points sequentially to 17%, and up 61 points y/y as the company achieved $22,000 of cost take-outs versus last year. We should point out the contrast here between Rivian and its next-largest rival, Lucid (LCID). While Lucid held on to its deliveries target for the year (at 20k vehicles, increasing at a ~2x y/y pace and about half of Rivian's scale), the company's gross margins weakened sequentially to -97% in Q1.

Of course, we should watch out for potential forward risk for Rivian: the company produced 14.6k vehicles in Q1 versus 8.6k in deliveries, so it is building up inventory that it will have to clear throughout Q2 and Q3. If production capacity falls to catch up to current demand levels, Rivian's gross margin could see downside pressure again.

We should note as well that Rivian expects some level of tariff impact to hurt its margins, particularly on batteries that it's importing from Korea. Per CFO Claire McDonough's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

We expect the per unit direct impact from tariffs to be a couple thousand dollars for 2025 based off of the currently announced tariffs in place. And as you can imagine, we're exploring a number of offsets to those cost increases, including strategic sourcing decisions as RJ made related to some of the batteries over the longer term and beyond those strategic sourcing decisions also have the opportunity to explore opportunities for us to see how we're deploying our incentive spend or drive improved mix which will help further offset some of the impact from tariffs themselves as well"

Still, the recent announcement of the U.S.-UK trade deal represents a major first step toward bilateral trade deals that reduce the impact of tariffs, and in my view, the announcement of further deals is a potential upside catalyst for Rivian in the near future.

Key takeaways

Rivian remains my preferred play in the EV space, as the company gears up for a very consequential R2 launch that introduces the Rivian brand at a mass-market price point. The company is making visible progress toward this launch, particularly in ramping up its newest production facility in Normal, Illinois and demonstrating consistent cost takeouts and gross margin expansion. I view weaker deliveries in FY25 as a normal pause ahead of a big product launch next year, and find Rivian well-positioned to sponge up demand from Tesla's recent declines. Stay long here and buy the dip.
